items.rationale: He views the sum-check protocol as the core technique that minimizes prover commitment costs and unlocks prover-time scalability; wants it to become the dominant SNARK design paradigm.

citations.quote: I hope the community adopts sum-check-based PIOPs combined with the Binius commitment for performant SNARKs
the right technique to unlock this is sum-check, due to its ability to minimize commitment costs for the prover

items.rationale: His own project; a 'paradigm shift' in zkVM design powered by sum-check and lookups that benchmarks faster than incumbents and is simpler/more extensible.

citations.quote: Early benchmarks indicate it outperforms RISC Zero by ~6x and SP1 by up to 2x. Major optimizations are still in the pipeline.
it instantiates the lookup singularity, yielding the simplest and most extensible zkVM to date

items.rationale: Believes a lookup-centric front-end yields simpler, more auditable SNARKs while being an order of magnitude faster than prior lookup arguments like Halo2's.

citations.quote: they realize @barrywhitehat's vision of a "lookup singularity" for simpler/more auditable SNARKs
Simplicity comes from lookups; performance from both.

items.rationale: Sees the Diamond & Posen refinement of Ligero/Brakedown, especially for small values and combined with sum-check, as reshaping hashing-based SNARKs and enabling fast SNARKs for hashes like Keccak.
items.age_note: Excitement builds across corpus: Brakedown (2023-08-31), D&P reshaping (2023-11-20), Binius endorsement (2024-06-03 = most recent).

citations.quote: work just unveiled by Ben Diamond and Jim Posen (D&P) that, in my view, reshapes the landscape for hashing-based SNARKs
I'm excited about this approach to polynomial commitments and hopeful we'll see further improvements soon

items.rationale: Despite favoring hashing-based small-field approaches, he insists fast elliptic-curve SNARKs remain essential for statements natively over large fields (signature verification, Verkle trees).

citations.quote: fast curve-based SNARKs (like the initial version of Jolt) will remain vital, especially for proving statements like signature verification and Verkle trees

items.rationale: Worries Fiat-Shamir on many-round FRI can lose security bits (no one had ruled it out), and that FRI over large 251/252-bit fields and high blowup imposes large prover/space costs versus sum-check approaches.
items.age_note: Most pointed critiques are 2023-07 through 2023-09; not restated or reversed in 2024 — 2024 statements simply favor sum-check/Binius instead.

citations.quote: Applying Fiat-Shamir to an r-round protocol with lambda bits of security can result in a non-interactive protocol with only lambda/r bits of security
using the 252-bit field is resulting in a 33x slowdown for the prover

items.rationale: Argues today's SNARKs were over-optimized for proof size, leaving prover time as the binding constraint; says a full reconfiguration of deployed stacks is needed to scale provers.

citations.quote: the scalability bottleneck for awhile now has been prover time
To achieve scalable SNARK provers, a complete reconfiguration of today's deployments is needed

items.rationale: Flags that recursion is inherently sequential and cannot be parallelized, so breaking computation into the tiniest pieces raises depth and becomes the real latency risk for rollup-scale proving.
items.age_note: Stated 2023-09-05; not restated later but never contradicted.

citations.quote: The real sequential bottleneck to be wary of is deep recursion. You can't parallelize that

items.rationale: Repeatedly concerned that the field conflates what projects actually do versus what they could do, and is imprecise in cost accounting, hindering progress and obscuring security tradeoffs.

citations.quote: the public discussion in this space has been deeply confused
I identify, and try to clear up, a variety of misconceptions that have been hindering progress and causing confusion

risk_regime.rationale: Thaler's statements are technical research positions on SNARK proof-system design (sum-check, lookups, polynomial commitments, zkVMs), not market, macro, or asset-risk commentary. He expresses strong structural conviction in a technology trajectory (sum-check + lookups + hashing-based commitments) but takes no stance on market risk appetite or token exposure.

notes: Corpus is ~99% cryptography research discourse from a a16z crypto researcher; no cryptocurrency tokens are discussed as investments, hence empty token arrays (dYdX/StarkEx and named projects like Polygon zkEVM, RISC Zero, StarkWare, Matter Labs, SP1 appear only as technical/security reference points, not investment views). All statements carry explicit 'not investment advice' disclaimers. Most-recent views (2024-04 to 2024-06) center on the Jolt release, exclusive use of sum-check-based PIOPs, the lookup singularity, and adoption of the Binius commitment — these dominate and are weighted highest. An evolution (not reversal) is visible on field size: in mid-2023 he stressed that large fields have important, non-artifact uses (ECDSA, RISC-V multiplication), while by late-2023/2024 he grew enthusiastic about small-field, hashing-based commitments (Binius/Brakedown/D&P); his current synthesized stance is both/and — small-field hashing approaches for performance, curve-based for large-field-native statements. Anti-FRI critiques are real but date to 2023 and are marked persisted.
